features.name: GKE Autopilot
features.description: A fully managed cluster configuration mode where Google provisions, configures, scales, and manages nodes, security, and operations.

features.name: Workload Identity
features.description: The recommended way for applications running in GKE to securely access Google Cloud services by mapping Kubernetes service accounts to IAM service accounts.

features.name: Binary Authorization
features.description: A deploy-time security control that ensures only trusted container images signed by authorized keys are deployed to GKE.

features.name: Cluster Autoscaler
features.description: Automatically resizes the GKE node pool based on pod resource demands, scaling down to zero when resources are idle.

iamRoles.description: Full access to GKE clusters, node pools, Kubernetes APIs, and Kubernetes configuration.
Allows managing Kubernetes deployments, pods, services, and workloads, but not modifying the cluster itself.
Read-only access to cluster configurations and nodes.

cliExamples.description: Create a regional GKE Autopilot cluster with private nodes and authorized networks
Get cluster credentials and configure kubectl

design: GKE Autopilot mode shifts node management, provisioning, scaling, and security patching to Google Cloud.
GKE Standard mode provides full control over the underlying VM nodes, custom machine types, and node pools.
Regional clusters distribute master replicas and nodes across three zones in a region, maximizing control plane SLA.
Zonal clusters run the master control plane and nodes in a single zone, exposing them to zonal failures.
Workload Identity eliminates the need to export and manage JSON service account keys in Kubernetes Secrets.
GKE integrates with Cloud Load Balancing, automatically configuring external Ingress via Google Front End (GFE).
Node pools allow grouping VMs with identical machine types and labels to schedule workloads on specific hardware.
Pod address ranges must be planned carefully during subnet design to avoid running out of IPs in private clusters.
security: Private GKE clusters hide node external IPs; nodes communicate with the internet via Cloud NAT.
Master Authorized Networks restricts access to the Kubernetes control plane API to specified CIDR blocks.
Kubernetes Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) manages fine-grained permissions inside the namespace, while IAM secures cluster control.
Binary Authorization enforces policies that prevent containers from running if they lack signatures from trusted build pipelines.
Shielded GKE nodes protect the host against boot-level rootkits by validating kernel signatures.
Enable Network Policies using Calico to restrict pod-to-pod communication inside the cluster (east-west traffic).
Encrypt Kubernetes Secrets at rest using keys managed by Cloud KMS (envelope encryption).
Container Threat Detection in Security Command Center monitors container runtime activity for suspicious behaviors.
reliability: Enable Node Auto-Repair so GKE automatically recreates nodes that fail health checks.
Enable Node Auto-Upgrade to keep nodes up-to-date with security patches and Kubernetes releases automatically.
Deploy workloads across multiple zones using Pod Anti-Affinity rules to ensure high availability.
Use Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to scale pod replicas based on CPU utilization or custom metrics.
Configure Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) to adjust CPU and memory requests/limits for pods automatically.
Maintain GKE cluster configuration and manifests in a Git repository to enable GitOps-based disaster recovery.
GKE Autopilot control plane SLA is 99.95% for regional clusters and 99.5% for zonal clusters.
Set pod resource requests and limits correctly so Kubernetes scheduler can distribute pods reliably across nodes.
cost: GKE Autopilot bills you based on the CPU, memory, and storage requested by your running pods, not node capacity.
GKE Standard bills for the underlying Compute Engine VMs, persistent disks, and load balancers running in your project.
A management fee of $0.10 per hour per cluster is charged, but GCP offers one free zonal cluster per billing account.
Use Spot VM node pools in GKE Standard for fault-tolerant, stateless batch workloads to save compute costs.
Configure Cluster Autoscaler to scale node pools down to zero when no pods are scheduled on them.
Utilize the GKE cost optimization dashboard in Cloud Console to identify overallocated CPU/RAM requests.
Choose Autopilot over Standard for low-overhead operational costs, reducing engineering hours spent on cluster management.
Establish resource quotas at the Kubernetes namespace level to prevent single teams from over-consuming cluster budget.
